docs.title: 1. Flash a Companion Device
docs.text: Configure a Companion device using the MeshCore Flasher Tool and connect it to your Raspberry Pi or Linux server via USB. Ensure radio settings are correct for your region.

docs.title: 2. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y python3 python3.12-venv curl git

docs.title: 3. Install the MeshCore Decoder
docs.text:
The MeshCore decoder is required for authentication when sending packets to the brokers:

curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.7/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc
nvm install --lts
npm install -g @michaelhart/meshcore-decoder

Verify the installation:

which meshcore-decoder

docs.title: 4. Run the meshcore-packet-capture Installer
docs.text:
bash <(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/agessaman/meshcore-packet-capture/main/install.sh)

The interactive installer will walk you through the remaining configuration steps below.

docs.title: 5. Device Connection
docs.text:
You will be prompted to select a connection type:

Method Best For Notes Serial (recommended) USB-connected devices Most reliable for continuous operation BLE T1000e and compatible devices Wireless, no cable needed TCP Network-connected devices For use with ser2net or TCP-to-serial bridges

For serial connections, the script will automatically detect connected devices. Select your device from the list.

docs.title: 6. IATA Code
docs.text: Enter your region's 3-letter IATA code (e.g., SEA, LAX, YOW, LON). This identifies which MeshMapper region your observer belongs to.

docs.title: 7. Owner Information (Optional)
docs.text:
You may optionally configure:

  • Owner Public Key: The public key of the observer owner (64 hex characters)
  • Owner Email: Contact email for the LetsMesh Analyzer

These are optional, apply to LetsMesh only, and can be skipped if you wish.

docs.title: LetsMesh Broker
docs.text: The script will ask whether to enable the LetsMesh Packet Analyzer. This connects to both the US and EU LetsMesh servers for redundancy. Recommended to enable.

docs.title: MeshMapper Broker
docs.text:
When prompted to configure additional brokers, select "1" additonal, and add the MeshMapper broker with the following settings:

Setting Value Server hostname mqtt.meshmapper.cc Port 443 WebSockets transport Yes TLS/SSL encryption Yes Verify TLS certificates Yes Authentication method MeshCore Auth Token Token audience mqtt.meshmapper.cc

When prompted for topic configuration, select Default pattern (meshcore/{IATA}/{PUBLIC_KEY}/status, meshcore/{IATA}/{PUBLIC_KEY}/packets).

docs.title: 9. Installation Method
docs.text:
Choose how the capture software should run:

Method Description System Service (recommended) Runs on boot via systemd, auto-restarts on failure Docker Container Isolated environment, easy to update Manual No automatic startup, run when needed

For the recommended systemd service, the script will:

  • Install and enable the service
  • Start it immediately
  • Verify a successful MQTT connection

docs.title: Managing the Service
docs.text:
Once installed as a systemd service, use these commands:

# Start / stop / restart
sudo systemctl start meshcore-capture
sudo systemctl stop meshcore-capture
sudo systemctl restart meshcore-capture

# Check status
sudo systemctl status meshcore-capture

# View live logs
sudo journalctl -u meshcore-capture -f

The configuration file is stored at:

~/.meshcore-packet-capture/.env.local

docs.title: Resource Limits
docs.text:
The systemd service includes built-in safeguards:

  • Memory: 512 MB limit
  • CPU: 50% limit
  • Tasks: 200 max
  • Auto-restart: Up to 3 failures within 5 minutes before stopping

docs.title: Verifying Your Observer
docs.text: Once your observer is running and connected, it will appear in your region's Admin Portal under the Observers tab once packets have been received (repeater or companion adverts, or wardriving pings) You should see a checkmark under the broker(s) your observer is connected to.

docs.title: Multi-Byte Repeaters
docs.text:
Every packet that travels through a MeshCore mesh network carries a record of its journey — a list of the repeaters it passed through. Each repeater stamps the packet with the prefix of its Public ID, called a hop.

MeshMapper fully supports multi-byte hops in the wardriving app, the map, and packet analyzer.

docs.title: The 1-Byte Problem
docs.text:
In 1-byte mode, repeaters are identified by just the first two hex characters of their Public ID (e.g., A1, 4F, 09). With only 254 possible combinations (hex 01 to FE — 00 and FF are reserved by MeshCore firmware), growing regions inevitably see two different repeaters sharing the same ID.

When this happens, MeshMapper can't tell which physical repeater was actually involved in a packet's path. It enters a quarantine mode for the affected devices to protect data integrity.

Learn More About Collisions

For a detailed explanation of how MeshMapper detects, handles, and resolves duplicate repeater IDs, see Duplicate Repeater IDs.

docs.title: Upgrading to Multi-Byte
docs.text: Upgrading a region from 1-byte to multi-byte hops is a gradual process — you don't need to update every device at once. Repeaters upgraded to the latest multi-byte capable firmware will still relay packets from repeaters and companions running older 1-byte firmware.

docs.title: Step 1: Update Firmware
docs.text:
Flash repeaters and companion devices with MeshCore firmware v1.14.1 or newer. The firmware's hop byte configuration must be set to 2 or 3 bytes.

In the command line console on the repeater, for 2-byte, set:

set path.hash.mode 1

and for 3-byte:

set path.hash.mode 2

When done, reboot the device, then send a flood advert. This will "upgrade" the repeater in MeshMapper to multi-byte capable automatically.

Repeaters and companions in the region should be running the same configuration.

docs.title: Step 2: Update Region Settings
docs.text: When the majority of a regions repeaters have been upgraded to a multi-byte capable firmware, in the region's admin panel, go to Settings and set the Hop Bytes value to match your regions configuration. Changing this value triggers an automatic collision recalculation across all repeaters in the region. (You must be an administrator of your region in order to access this setting)

docs.title: Step 3: Automatic Detection and Upgrade
docs.text:
As updated repeaters relay packets, MeshMapper detects the longer hop IDs automatically. It tracks hop bytes per repeater — so each device's byte length updates individually as it's heard on the network.

For example, if repeater A1B2C3D4E5F6 was previously known as a 1-byte device (hop ID A1), the first time it's heard with a 2-byte hop (A1B2), MeshMapper updates its record. No manual intervention needed.

Mixed Environments

During a transition period, a region may have a mix of 1-byte and 2/3-byte repeaters. MeshMapper handles this gracefully — it uses each repeater's individually tracked hop byte value for collision detection and path resolution. Two repeaters that collide at 1 byte may be perfectly distinguishable at 2 bytes.

Packet Types

Not all packet types are ingested into the MeshMapper engine responsible for processing repeater upgrades. In order for a repeater to automatically upgrade to 2 or 3-byte capable within MeshMapper, its ID must be contained within the path of an advert or wardriving packet. Public messages or other packet types (trace, request, etc.) will not result in an automatic upgrade.

docs.title: Smart Collision Resolution
docs.text:
As repeaters upgrade, collisions that existed in 1-byte mode may automatically resolve:

  • Repeater AB (1-byte) and repeater AB (1-byte) are in collision — indistinguishable.
  • After upgrading, repeater AB12 (2-byte) and repeater AB9F (2-byte) are no longer in collision — the longer IDs differentiate them.

MeshMapper re-evaluates collisions as hop bytes update, restoring previously excluded repeaters to active status when they become distinguishable.

docs.title: Filtering by Hop Bytes
docs.text: The map's advanced filter panel includes a Hop Bytes filter, allowing you to view only repeaters of a specific byte length. This is helpful for tracking firmware upgrade progress across your region — quickly see which repeaters are still running 1-byte firmware and need updating. In addition, a list of repeaters and their byte modes/capabilities can be found in the Region Info page under Repeaters.

docs.title: Multiregion Groups
docs.text: A Multiregion Group allows multiple individual regions/IATA's to be combined into a single, seamless map interface, and allows MeshMapper to properly represent data based on real-world conditions.

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text:
When regions are grouped:

  1. Unified Map: The individual maps and leaderboards for each region are effectively merged.
  2. Shared Logic: The system treats the group as a single entity for data processing.
    • Duplicate Detection: Scans for duplicate repeater IDs across all regions in the group. If region AAA has a repeater AA and region BBB has a repeater AA, those repeaters will correctly enter collision with one another.
    • Leaderboards: Statistics are aggregated. A user's contribution counts towards the group leaderboard, and repeaters are ranked against all others in the group.
  3. Centralized Administration: Administrators can gain access to a Grouped Admin Panel, allowing them to monitor and manage all sub-regions simultaneously. Individual regions within the group can still be administered individually.
  4. Wardriving Handoff: Wardrivers leaving one individual region and entering another will have their session automatically transferred and companion authorized without interruption.

docs.title: When to use a Multiregion Group
docs.text: Multiregion groups are designed for specific scenarios where one regions mesh traffic can be heard by another.

docs.title: ✅ When to use multiregion groups
docs.text:
• When repeaters in one region can be heard by observers in a neighboring region.
  • When wardriving in one region may have pings heard/repeated by repeaters in a neighboring region.

docs.title: ❌ When NOT to use it
docs.text: • When repeater adverts or wardriving traffic in one region would not be heard by repeaters or observers in another. Combining two regions that cannot physically share mesh traffic can result in repeaters unnecessarily entering a collision/duplicate state.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
The primary reason to create multiregion groups is data accuracy. Use this example below:

In this example, you have a car wardriving around the border of region AAA. Nearby is region BBB.

The wardrivers companion sends out a ping. Region AAA's repeater AA is too far away to hear the ping. Region BBB's repeater AA is close enough and rebroadcasts that ping. The wardrivers companion hears this repeat. The ping propagates through the mesh network is is picked up by an observer in region AAA. MeshMapper's backend processes both data points and determines successful bidirectional communication...

  • ❌ When the regions ARE NOT grouped: ...but incorrectly associates it with region AAA's repeater AA (it is the only AA it knows of). A link from that grid square to the incorrect repeater is made.
  • ✅ When the regions ARE grouped: ...however, it is aware of two repeaters sharing the ID of AA. Associations ARE NOT made to either repeater as the data is ambiguous. The map will draw suggestion lines from that grid square to both repeaters and provide signal strength information, which may allow the viewer to make a reasonable assumption on their own as to which repeater was actually involved.

docs.title: Administration
docs.text: Managing a multiregion group is similar to managing a single region, with added capabilities.

docs.title: Grouped Admin Panel
docs.text: Administrators can view data for the entire group or filter by specific sub-regions. * Global Actions: Tools like "Replace Repeater" or "Bulk Update" can apply to the entire group. * Notes: Adding a note to a repeater or companion (e.g., "Tom owns this repeater") makes that note visible to all admins in the group, regardless of which specific region the repeater belongs to.

docs.title: Requesting a Multiregion Group
docs.text: As each region in a multiregion group still maintains its own backend databases, regions can be added or removed from multiregion groups easily. Please reach out to a Global Administrator to start the process.

docs.title: Onboarding a New Region
docs.text: MeshMapper is designed to be scalable, allowing new geographic regions to be added to the network. This guide outlines the process for community members to request a new map zone for their local mesh.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before requesting a new region, you must ensure the local infrastructure is ready to support it.

  • Active Mesh Community: There should be an active group of users in the area.
  • MQTT Observers: You need at least one (preferably 2-3) nodes configured as MQTT Clients connected to the LetsMesh broker, the MeshMapper broker, or both.
    • These nodes act as the "ears" of the map, reporting traffic to MeshMapper.
    • See MeshMapper MQTT Setup for detailed instructions.

docs.title: The Onboarding Form
docs.text:
To start the process, navigate to the Region Onboarding form (accessed via the About page).

The form collects the following critical information:

Field Description IATA Code The 3-letter code that will identify your region in the database and URL (e.g., yow.meshmapper.net). Region Name The display name for the map (e.g., "Ottawa, CA", "London, UK"). Region Radius A rough estimate (in km) of the area you intend to cover. Contact Info Your Discord username or Email address. This is required for administrators to contact you during the setup process. Public Channels A list of public channels used in your mesh (e.g., Chat, Emergency). This helps the wardriving app correctly identify valid traffic. Observer IDs The Public ID (e.g., 12345678XXXXXX...) of the nodes acting as MQTT gateways. Volunteer as Administrator Optional. Tick this to volunteer as your region's administrator. Requires Discord to be linked. See below for details.

docs.title: Volunteer as Region Administrator
docs.text:
The onboarding form includes an optional Volunteer as Region Administrator checkbox.

Enabling this option signals to the MeshMapper team that you are willing to take on the administrator role for your new region. As a region administrator, you would be responsible for:

  • Monitoring and managing your region's data and settings.
  • Supporting local wardrivers and answering questions about the map.
  • Being an active and available point of contact for your mesh community.

Requirements:

  • You must link your Discord account before this option becomes available. The checkbox is disabled until Discord is connected.
  • You must be genuinely active in your local mesh community.

What happens if you volunteer:

When your region is approved and deployed, an administrator account will be automatically created for you. Your credentials (username and a generated key) will be sent to you via Discord DM. You should log in to your region's admin panel and change your key immediately after first login.

If you already have an administrator account on another MeshMapper region, access to the new region will simply be added to your existing account.

docs.title: Defining the Boundary
docs.text:
One of the most important steps is defining the geographic boundary of your region.

  • The Map Tool: The form includes an interactive map with drawing tools.
  • Draw Polygon: Use the Polygon Tool (pentagon icon) to draw a precise shape around your mesh's coverage area if a circle doesn't accurately describe it. Alternatively, click the "Auto Generate Boundry" button to have AI attempt to draw the boundry for you.
  • Import GeoJSON: Alternatively, click the Import GeoJSON button to paste GeoJSON data directly. This is useful if you already have a boundary defined in another tool (e.g., geojson.io). Supported formats include Polygon, MultiPolygon, Feature, and FeatureCollection.
  • Region Center: The center pin automatically moves to the center of the polygon when one is drawn or imported. You can also drag the pin manually if needed. This determines where your region appears on the global map.
  • Purpose: This polygon is used to:
    • Define where wardriving activities in your region can occur.
    • Determine if a user is "In Zone" for authentication purposes.
    • Render the region border on the global map.

Note: Keep the boundary reasonable. You can always expand it later as your network grows.

docs.title: Submission & Approval
docs.text:
Once you submit the form:

    1. MQTT Verification: MeshMapper servers will verify if MQTT-connected observers are sending data to the brokers at either letsmesh.net or MeshMapper (or both).
    1. Review: If the servers are able to verify active observers, the region is presented to MeshMapper administrators for review. Administrators will ensure the region isn't a duplicate, is named correctly, notes entered during onboarding are reviewed, etc.
    1. Activation: Once reviewed, the region is approved and will become available for wardriving. It can take up to 5 minutes after deployment for the MeshMapper MQTT engine to start pulling in local data.

If you associated your Discord account during the onboarding process, you will receive automatic messages from MeshMapper with onboarding status.

Request Deletion

Pending onboarding requests that have not passed MQTT verification in 3 days will automatically be deleted.

docs.title: Legacy Data
docs.text: If you have historical coverage data from other systems, it can be imported into MeshMapper. This data will appear on a separate layer and is not included in leaderboard statistics. See Data Upload for format requirements.

docs.title: Overriding Duplicate ID Detection
docs.text:
MeshMapper's default behavior is to aggressively quarantine repeaters and pings with colliding ID's to ensure map accuracy (see Duplicate Repeater IDs). However, some regions may prefer to visualize all repeaters and connections, even if the data attribution is technically ambiguous.

The Disable Duplicate ID Detection Logic setting allows regions to bypass these safety checks.

Data Integrity Warning

Enabling this setting compromises the accuracy of the map and data.

Because the system cannot distinguish between two repeaters sharing the same ID (e.g., A1), coverage lines may be drawn to the wrong location, and "Max Range" statistics may be falsely inflated.

docs.title: How to Enable
docs.text:
1. Region administrators can log in to the Admin Portal.
  2. Navigate to the Settings tab.
  3. Check the box for Disable Duplicate ID Detection Logic.
  4. Click Save Settings.

Note: If your region is part of a Multi-Region Group, this setting must be enabled by a Master or Global Administrator.

docs.title: Changes to System Behavior
docs.text: When this mode is active, the system alters its logic in the following ways:

docs.title: 1. Map Visualization
docs.text:
• Icons: Repeaters involved in a collision will no longer display the red Excluded icon. They will appear as standard Active repeaters (Orange/Grey/Green).
  • Coverage Lines: The map will draw connection lines from pings to all repeaters that match the ID, even if the ping data is ambiguous.
  • Neighbor Lines: Neighbor lines will be drawn for every repeater sharing a colliding ID.
  • Public Warning: A warning icon will appear on the public map interface (below the Help button) to inform visitors that the region has opted out of standard validation logic.

docs.title: 2. Data Ingestion
docs.text:
• Persistence: The system will no longer automatically force existing repeaters into "Excluded" status when a collision is detected. If you manually set a duplicate repeater to Active, it will remain Active.
  • New Collisions: To ensure admins are still aware of issues, newly discovered repeaters that cause a collision will still default to "Excluded" initially. However, unlike the standard mode, you can manually change them to "Active" without the system reverting your change on the next ping. Regardless of being "Excluded", the repeater will still function as being "Active".

docs.title: 3. Leaderboards
docs.text:
• Local Leaderboard: Duplicate repeaters will be included in the regional "Best Repeaters" and "Max Range" tables.
  • Global Leaderboard: To protect the integrity of the global rankings, regions with this setting enabled are excluded from the Global Leaderboard. Your repeaters will not appear.

docs.title: Summary of Differences
docs.text: Feature Standard Mode Override Mode Duplicate Repeater Status Forced to "Excluded" (Red) Can be manually set to "Active" Ambiguous Pings Ignored / Not drawn Drawn to all matching candidates Neighbor Lines Drawn only for unique IDs Drawn for all colliding IDs Max Range Stats Calculated only for verified unique IDs Calculated for all IDs (may be inaccurate) Global Leaderboard Included Excluded

docs.title: Privacy Notice
docs.text:
Last Updated: March 23, 2026

MeshMapper ("we", "us", or "our") operates the MeshMapper platform at meshmapper.net and its associated regional subdomains. This Privacy Notice explains how we collect, use, and protect your information when you use our services.

docs.title: Account Information
docs.text: When you associate via Discord OAuth, we receive your Discord user ID, username, and avatar. We use this solely for automated notification purposes.

docs.title: Location and Coverage Data
docs.text:
When you participate in wardriving sessions, the following data is collected:

  • GPS coordinates (latitude and longitude) of your device during active sessions
  • Signal metrics such as RSSI, SNR, and hop counts
  • Session metadata including timestamps, region, and session identifiers
  • Node identifiers for mesh network repeaters and devices encountered, including your device name

This data is essential to the core function of MeshMapper: building community-driven RF coverage maps.

docs.title: Technical Data
docs.text:
We may collect standard technical information such as:

  • IP addresses (used for rate limiting and abuse prevention)
  • Browser user-agent strings
  • Server access logs

docs.title: How We Use Your Information
docs.text:
We use the information we collect to:

  • Provide the service: Display RF coverage maps, leaderboards, and network analytics
  • Manage sessions: Track active wardriving sessions and associate data with contributors
  • Improve the platform: Analyze aggregate usage patterns to improve map accuracy and features
  • Prevent abuse: Rate limiting, session validation, and security monitoring

docs.title: Data Sharing and Visibility
docs.text:
• Coverage data is public. GPS-tagged signal data submitted through wardriving is displayed on publicly accessible maps. This is the core purpose of MeshMapper.
  • Leaderboard data is public. Usernames and contribution statistics appear on regional and global leaderboards (if "anonymous" mode is not enabled on the wardriving app).
  • We do not sell your personal data to third parties.
  • We do not share your data with advertisers.
  • We may share data with regional administrators who manage their respective MeshMapper regions.

docs.title: Data Storage and Security
docs.text:
• All data is stored in databases on our servers.
  • Sessions are managed server-side using PHP sessions and API key authentication.
  • MQTT-ingested wardriving packets are encrypted with AES and decrypted server-side.
  • We take reasonable measures to protect your data, but no method of electronic transmission or storage is 100% secure.

docs.title: Third-Party Services
docs.text:
MeshMapper integrates with the following third-party services:

  • Discord for OAuth authentication (Discord Privacy Policy)
  • Leaflet as the map rendering engine (Leaflet)
  • CARTO for dark mode map tiles (CARTO Privacy Policy)
  • OpenTopoMap for topography tiles (OpenTopoMap)
  • Esri for satellite imagery — Source: Esri, i-cubed, USDA, USGS, AEX, GeoEye, Getmapping, Aerogrid, IGN, IGP, UPR-GP, and the GIS User Community (Esri Privacy Policy)
  • REST Countries for country code lookup during region onboarding (REST Countries)
  • Nominatim for geocoding / location search (Nominatim Usage Policy)
  • OpenStreetMap for map data (OpenStreetMap Privacy Policy)
  • LetsMesh.net as the MQTT broker for wardriving data ingestion (LetsMesh)

docs.title: Data Retention
docs.text:
• Coverage and session data is retained indefinitely to maintain historical map accuracy.
  • You may request deletion of your data by contacting us at admin@meshmapper.net.

docs.title: Your Rights
docs.text:
Depending on your jurisdiction, you may have the right to:

  • Access the personal data we hold about you
  • Correct inaccurate or incomplete data
  • Delete your personal data (subject to legitimate retention needs)
  • Object to or restrict certain processing of your data

To exercise any of these rights, please contact us at admin@meshmapper.net.

docs.title: Children's Privacy
docs.text: MeshMapper is not directed at children. We do not knowingly collect personal information from children. If you believe a child has provided us with personal data, please contact us at admin@meshmapper.net so we can remove it.

docs.title: Changes to This Notice
docs.text: We may update this Privacy Notice from time to time. Changes will be reflected by updating the "Last Updated" date at the top of this page.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: If you have questions about this Privacy Notice, please reach out via the MeshMapper Discord community or contact us at admin@meshmapper.net.

docs.title: Report Bugs & Request Features
docs.text:
MeshMapper is actively being developed using community feedback, and bugs are expected.

You can submit a ticket easily using our guided form:

Submit Bug or Feature Request

Alternatively, you can report bugs directly on the MeshMapper Central Issue Tracking and Project Planning Github repo here:

https://github.com/MeshMapper/MeshMapper_Project

We also welcome ideas and suggestions to improve MeshMapper. MeshMapper is a community-driven product and all feedback is welcome.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: If you're having trouble with MeshMapper, you're in the right place. Browse the common issues below, or use the resources at the bottom of this page to get further help.

docs.title: Map & Coverage
docs.text:
My repeater isn't showing on the map.

Repeaters appear on the map once their adverts are heard through a connected observer. Try the following:

  • Force an advert from your repeater.
  • Confirm the region has at least one active observer by checking Region Info > Observers from the map. A repeaters advert must be picked up by one of these observers. If observers are missing, contact your region's administrator, as they may have a whitelist of observers from which to ingest from configured.
  • If the repeater or observer was recently added, give it a few minutes to be picked up.
  • Ensure the repeater has location coordinates configured.
  • The region may have the option "New Repeaters Enter Pending State" enabled (check through the Region Info page), in which event the region administrator will need to approve the repeater before it is added.
  • Repeaters that are well outside of a regions defined radius may not get added to the map as an anti-spam/anti-misconfiguration measure. Please check with your region's administrator.

If your repeater or room server is also an observer, it may not "hear" its own advert. Another observer must be within range in order to ingest it's advert and display on the map. If there are no other observers nearby, you can mimic the repeaters advert from a companion by using the MeshCore app, select the ellipses beside the repeater, select "Share", and then select "Zero Hop Advert". When this is the case, however, this repeater will eventually become stale and get removed. This process must be repeated regularly.

If it still doesn't appear, contact your region's administrator.

My repeater shows as Excluded or Duplicate.

As a region's mesh network grows, the likelihood of two repeaters sharing the same short ID increases. MeshMapper detects these collisions and disables affected repeaters to protect data integrity. See Duplicate Repeater IDs for a full explanation and resolution steps.

The map is slow or unresponsive on my device.

Large regions with lots of data can be demanding on older devices or slow connections. Try the following:

  • Coverage Only Mode: A "Switch to Coverage Only" button appears on the loading screen. This loads pre-rendered tiles instead of interactive data, significantly reducing resource usage.
  • Increase grid size: In Settings, increase the grid size (e.g. from 100m to 300m or larger) to reduce the number of rendered elements.
  • Filter by time: Use Advanced Search to limit data to a recent time range (e.g. Last 30 Days).
  • Grid Mode: Use the "Simplified" grid mode from within the map settings.
Grid squares are showing unexpected colours.

Grid square colours depend on the active Coverage Mode:

  • Standard: Colours represent ping type (green=BIDIR, orange=TX, purple=RX, etc.)
  • Effective Coverage: Averaged quality score (green=high quality, red=low quality)
  • Signal Strength: Based on SNR (green=strong, red=weak)
  • Ping Age: Based on data recency (green=recent, red=stale)
  • Noise Floor: Based on RF noise relative to each companion's baseline (green=quiet, red=loud)

Check which mode is selected in the Coverage Mode section of the Layer Control.

docs.title: Mobile App
docs.text:
The app won't connect to my MeshCore device.

Bluetooth connections can be finicky. Try the following:

  1. Ensure Bluetooth is enabled on your phone.
  2. Make sure your MeshCore device is powered on and not connected to another app.
  3. Try toggling Bluetooth off and on again.
  4. Restart the MeshMapper app.
  5. If pairing fails repeatedly, remove the device from your phone's Bluetooth settings and re-pair.

See the Connection Guide for detailed pairing instructions.

The app says I'm outside of a region.

You need to be within a registered MeshMapper region to start a wardriving session. If there isn't a region near you, you can request one.

GPS isn't locking or accuracy is poor.

GPS accuracy depends on your device and environment. Try the following:

  • Ensure location permissions are granted to the MeshMapper app.
  • Move to an area with a clear view of the sky (away from tall buildings or dense tree cover).
  • Wait a minute or two for the GPS to get a solid fix before starting a session.
  • On Android, ensure location mode is set to "High accuracy."

docs.title: Administration
docs.text:
I can't access the admin portal.

The admin portal is only available to region administrators. If you believe you should have access, confirm with the person who set up the region or contact a Moderator on Discord. See the Administrator List to find your region's admin.

Observer data isn't coming through.

If your observer isn't forwarding data:

  1. Check that the observer device is running and connected to the MQTT broker.
  2. Verify the MQTT topic and credentials in your observer configuration.
  3. Check the Observers panel in Region Info to see if the observer is listed and its last-heard timestamp.

See MeshMapper MQTT Setup for configuration details.

docs.title: Getting More Help
docs.text:
If you can't find the answer here, there are several ways to get support:

  • Wiki: Browse the full wiki documentation.
  • AI Bot (@MeshMapper): The MeshMapper AI bot on Discord can answer many questions. Send it a DM, tag it in a public message, or reply to a message and tag it.
  • Discord Community: The MeshMapper community on Discord is knowledgeable and very helpful. Post your question in the appropriate channel and someone will be able to help you out.
  • Region Administrator: For issues specific to your region (settings, configuration, etc.), reach out to your region's administrator. If they're unavailable, tag a @Moderator in the MeshMapper Discord server. Moderators (also known as Global Administrators) have access to assist any region.

docs.title: Reporting Bugs
docs.text: Notice a bug? You can submit a ticket using the guided form on the Report Bugs & Features page, or tag the MeshMapper AI bot on Discord with !bug followed by a description.

docs.title: Feature Requests
docs.text:
MeshMapper was built using suggestions from the community. If you have an idea, tag the AI bot with !feature followed by your suggestion, or submit it through the website or mobile app.

Due to the size and activity of the community, these are the only methods to submit tickets. The developers may miss the request if sent any other way.

docs.title: Map Grid, Ping Types & Popups
docs.text: MeshMapper visualizes mesh coverage using a grid system, colour-coded markers, and detailed popups. This guide explains how to interpret the map data.

docs.title: The Grid System
docs.text:
The map is divided into small grid squares. The default grid size is 300m x 300m in Simplified mode and 100m x 100m in Detailed mode, but this can be changed via the Grid Size option in the Settings panel (sizes range from 50m up to 2km).

Since a single grid square may contain multiple pings from different times or users, MeshMapper uses a Priority System to determine which colour to display. The map will always show the "best" result available for that location.

Priority Colour Meaning 1 (Highest) Green BIDIR (Bidirectional) - Confirmed two-way coverage. 2 Cyan DISC (Discovery) - Discovery packet sent and reply heard. 3 Orange TX (Transmit) - Message sent, but no repeat was heard. 4 Purple RX (Receive) - Heard traffic while in RX mode. 5 Grey DEAD - Repeater heard the ping, but it did not make it into the wider mesh 6 (Lowest) Red DROP - Failed ping. No repeats heard, did not make it into the wider mesh.

Example: If a grid square contains 10 failed pings (Red) and 1 successful ping (Green), the square will appear Green to indicate that coverage is possible at that location.

docs.title: Ping Types Defined
docs.text:
Here is a detailed breakdown of the different coverage statuses:

  • BIDIRECTIONAL (BIDIR) - Green

    • You sent a message, it was received by a repeater, and your radio heard the repeater send it back.
    • This confirms a solid two-way link.

  • DISCOVERY (DISC) - Cyan

    • Your radio sent a specialized "Discovery" packet, and a node responded.

  • TRANSMIT (TX) - Orange

    • Your message was successfully sent into the mesh (it reached a repeater), but your radio did not hear the confirmation repeat.
    • This often happens when you have a high-power transmitter but a lower-gain antenna for receiving, or if the repeater is far away.

  • RECEIVE (RX) - Purple

    • Your radio heard packets from the mesh (e.g., other users, telemetry), but you did not transmit.
    • Useful for mapping where the mesh can be heard, even if you cannot reach it.

  • DEAD - Grey

    • A repeater heard your signal, but the message didn't go anywhere (it wasn't repeated to the rest of the mesh).
    • This usually indicates a "Zombie" repeater, a misconfigured repeater that is receiving but not routing, or the repeater does not have a reliable connection into the wider mesh.

  • DROP - Red

    • You sent a message, but no repeaters heard it, and no route was established.
    • Indicates a dead zone or lack of coverage.

docs.title: Popups & Data
docs.text: Clicking on a grid square opens a popup with detailed information.

docs.title: 1. Summary View
docs.text: If a grid square contains multiple pings, the popup opens in Summary View first. - Counts: Shows the total number of pings for each type (BIDIR, TX, etc.) in that square. - Averages: Displays the Average SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) and Average Noise Floor. - Max Range: Shows the furthest distance achieved from a repeater in this square.

docs.title: 2. Detail View
docs.text:
You can cycle through individual pings using the < and > buttons at the bottom of the popup, arranged by the newest data first.

  • Identity: Who sent the ping (if not hidden via region settings).
  • Time: Date and time of the ping.
  • Telemetry:
    • Power: Transmit power used (e.g., 0.6W).
    • Ext. Ant: Indicates if an external antenna was used.
  • Path:
    • Heard Repeats: A list of all repeaters your radio heard re-broadcasting your message, sorted by signal strength (SNR).
    • Via: The first repeaters responsible for transmitting your coverage data to MeshMapper.

docs.title: 3. Visual Lines
docs.text:
When a popup is open, MeshMapper draws lines on the map to visualize the connection:

  • Solid Coloured Line: Represents the signal your radio Heard from a repeater. The colour indicates signal strength (Green = Good, Orange = Okay, Red = Weak).
  • Blue Dashed Line: Represents the one-way Via path.
  • Red Dashed Line: Indicates a connection to a Duplicate/Excluded repeater.

docs.title: 4. Sharing
docs.text: Every popup includes a Link Icon (chain link) in the top-right corner. Clicking this copies a direct URL to that specific grid square or ping to your clipboard, allowing you to share exact locations with others.

docs.title: Repeater Icons
docs.text:
The icons representing repeaters on the map change colour based on their status:

  • Pink: Active. The repeater is active and online.
  • Orange: New. The repeater was discovered by MeshMapper within the last 14 days.
  • Grey: Stale. The repeater has not sent an advert within the predetermined timeframe (set by the regional administrators).
  • Red: Excluded. The repeater is flagged as a duplicate or has been manually flagged as excluded.

docs.title: Repeater Popups
docs.text:
Clicking on a repeater icon opens a popup with details:

  • Status Indicators:
    • Online (Green): The repeater has been heard from recently.
    • New (Orange): First seen within the last 14 days.
    • Stale (Grey): Has not been heard from in a specific timeframe set by the region administrator (e.g., 24 hours).
    • Excluded (Red): Flagged as a duplicate or problematic node.
  • Details:
    • ID: The repeaters identifier.
    • First Heard: The date the repeater was first discovered by MeshMapper.
    • Last Heard: The exact time the repeater was last seen by the mesh.
    • Max Range: The furthest distance a user has successfully connected to this repeater from.
  • Neighbours: A list of other repeaters this node has directly communicated with recently.
  • Copy Link: A link icon in the top right allows you to copy a direct URL to this repeater.

docs.title: Private Repeaters
docs.text:
If a repeater's name ends with the "no entry" emoji (🚫), it is treated as a Private Repeater:

  • Map: The repeater's Name, Location, and ID are completely removed from the map.
  • Pings: Coverage data is still kept and displayed on the grid, but the repeater's identity in the popup is masked as (hidden).
  • Leaderboards: The repeater's name is replaced with "(private repeater)", though its statistics are still calculated and ranked.

docs.title: Webhooks
docs.text:
Webhooks allow MeshMapper to send real-time notifications to any external HTTPS endpoint when events occur in your region. This is useful for integrating with services like Home Assistant, custom bots, or any system that can receive HTTP POST requests.

Webhooks work alongside Discord DM notifications — enabling a webhook does not disable Discord notifications.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the Admin Portal → Settings tab for your region.
  2. In the Webhook Notifications section, enter your webhook URL.
  3. Select which events should trigger a webhook notification.
  4. Click Save Settings.
  5. Use the Send Test button to verify your endpoint receives the test payload.

HTTPS Required

Only HTTPS URLs are accepted. HTTP, internal, and private network URLs are blocked for security.

docs.title: Events
docs.text:
You can enable or disable each event type individually:

Event Description Duplicate Repeater ID A new repeater has been detected with an ID that collides with an existing repeater, putting both into Excluded status. Pending Repeater Your region has repeaters in Pending state awaiting admin review. Checked once daily. Offline Observer One or more MQTT observers in your region may be offline. Checked once daily. Visitor Message A visitor has sent a message to your region's administrators via the map.

By default, all events are enabled when a webhook URL is configured.

docs.title: Payload Format
docs.text:
Every webhook sends an HTTP POST request with a JSON body. The structure is consistent across all event types:

{
  "event": "duplicate_repeater",
  "region": "YOW",
  "timestamp": 1744123456,
  "message": "New ID collision detected on YOW.\n\nCollision Group: A1\n\nRepeaters:\nRepeaterA (A1B2C3D4)\nRepeaterB (A1E5F6G7)",
  "data": {
    "collision_group": "A1",
    "repeaters": ["RepeaterA (A1B2C3D4)", "RepeaterB (A1E5F6G7)"]
  }
}

docs.title: Fields
docs.text:
• event — The event type (e.g., duplicate_repeater, pending_repeater, offline_observer, visitor_message, test).
  • region — The region code (e.g., YOW).
  • timestamp — Unix timestamp of when the event occurred.
  • message — A human-readable summary of the event. This is the same text sent via Discord DM, so simple integrations can use it directly.
  • data — Event-specific structured data (see below).

docs.title: Event-Specific Data
docs.text:
duplicate_repeater:

  • collision_group — The ID that collided (e.g., A1).
  • repeaters — Array of repeater names and IDs involved in the collision.

pending_repeater:

  • count — Number of repeaters currently in Pending state.

offline_observer:

  • stale_observers — Array of observer descriptions that appear to be offline.

visitor_message:

  • body — The full text of the visitor's message.

test:

  • info — A static test string confirming the webhook is working.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Webhook not firing:

  • Ensure the URL starts with https://.
  • Verify the event type checkbox is enabled in Settings.
  • Check that the URL is reachable from the MeshMapper server.
  • Use the Send Test button to isolate the issue.

Test button shows "Failed":

  • The endpoint may be unreachable, returning an error status code, or timing out (3-second limit).
  • Verify the URL is correct and the endpoint is accepting POST requests with JSON content.

Receiving duplicate notifications:

  • Webhooks and Discord DMs operate independently. If you receive both, this is expected behavior.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
• Webhook requests use HTTP POST with Content-Type: application/json.
  • Requests have a 3-second timeout and do not follow redirects.
  • Failed webhook deliveries do not retry.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Submit Feedback — New button under Settings lets you submit bug reports and feature requests directly from the app.
  • Upload Debug Logs — Send debug logs privately to the dev team. Attaching logs to a bug report works the same way.
  • Full Repeater Key Capture — Discovery requests now capture complete repeater public keys. Coming soon: coverage mapping for duplicate-ID repeaters based on discovery data.

docs.title: 🔧 Improvements
docs.text: • Removed Legacy Path Filter — Removed an early filter that dropped RX packets with path lengths over 9. This was a quick fix for filtering corrupt packets in Ottawa but didn't scale to larger meshes. It's now replaced by proper decode/decrypt validation with ASCII readability checks. One user's 1-hour session was losing 30 RX pings due to this filter.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text: • Fixed parser logic that failed to validate messages and adverts containing emojis, which was causing dropped packets.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• DISC Mapping Complete — DISC packets now appear on the leaderboard and map. Collision logic has been updated: while BIDIR/TX/RX remain hidden for collided repeaters, DISC pings will still show. DISC pings now link to repeaters via their full public key, bypassing duplicate ID logic.
  • Hidden Repeaters — Any repeater with the 🚫 emoji in its name is now removed from the map. Associated pings display as "hidden" (no ID shown), but statistics like max distance and coverage level remain. The leaderboard still lists the repeater, but with the name stripped. This aligns with the MeshCore official app behavior.
  • Configurable Stale Timer — The stale repeater timeout is now adjustable per region and multi-region group (previously hardcoded to 24h). These settings can be found in the region and master admin panels.
  • Copy Link Button — Ping and repeater popups now feature a clipboard icon. Clicking it copies a direct link to that specific grid square or repeater, making it easier to share locations instead of screenshots.

docs.title: 🔧 Improvements & Fixes
docs.text: • Various smaller fixes and performance improvements.

docs.title: 📱 MeshMapper Native App v1.0.0 (1769835681)
docs.text:
Forced Update

Please update your app immediately. All previous versions have been disabled. We discovered critical bugs that were incorrectly marking successful bidirectional pings as DEAD tiles.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• SEA Region Configuration & Session Limit — SEA was mistakenly configured to allow zero Active sessions, but a recent app bug was ignoring this limit entirely. This let everyone Active wardrive when they shouldn't have, causing successful pings to appear as DEAD. Both the server config and app have been corrected.
  • API Queue Fix — A recent simplification to the app's API queue had unintended consequences causing DEAD pings in BNA. This has been reverted/fixed.
  • Infinite Session Fix — If the app was backgrounded, it could keep calling its heartbeat forever. Now the app properly stops heartbeat calls when disconnected from BLE or backgrounded and not in use.
  • Minor IATA Display Bug — Visual only, wasn't causing data issues, but now fixed.
  • Clearer Bluetooth Messaging — You'll now see a proper "Bluetooth required" message when trying to connect, and the scan button is hidden when BLE is disabled.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Non-onboarded regions now show the closest available region on the Connect tab.
  • Replaced the 25m movement requirement for Send Ping with a 15-second cooldown instead, as voted by the community.
  • (Android) Added exit options: auto-exit on BLE disconnect and a manual close button to fully shut down the app.
  • Added "Map Controls" section to the Legend & Info popup (Thanks @Uncle Lit).

docs.title: 🔧 Improvements
docs.text:
• Added more debug logging around GPS for better troubleshooting.
  • App now detects missing internet connection and notifies you (Thanks @Uncle Lit).
  • Map toggles can now be minimized in portrait mode.
  • Renamed "Map Legend" to "Legend & Info".
  • Added visual feedback to sound preview buttons in Legend & Info.
  • Dropped packets due to suspected carpeater (RSSI of -30 or stronger) are now logged to the error log for visibility.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed onboarding URL on Android not launching properly.
  • Fixed an issue where corrupted app data caused the app to hang indefinitely and prevented connections. The app now auto-recovers by resetting the corrupted data and logs a message if this occurs. Huge thanks to @Dreikor for troubleshooting.
  • Fixed cooldown timers getting stuck at 1s for a few seconds before completing.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Legacy Data Integration — Developed a tool to ingest historical data via CSV import. Uploaded data now populates a dedicated "Legacy Data" layer on the map.
    • Note: This is currently a "hidden" tool while we assess data submissions. It will shortly become available for region administrators. If you have legacy data to migrate, please reach out directly.
  • Unit Preferences — Users can now toggle between Imperial and Metric units directly from the settings icon on the map interface or leaderboards.
  • "Subscribe to All" Observers — A new option is available during onboarding and within region admin panels. When enabled, this overrides manual MQTT observer ID input and automatically subscribes the region to all letsmesh observers in their IATA. Manual entry is still supported for specific exclusions.

docs.title: 🕰️ Previous Updates (Now Documented)
docs.text:
• Discovery Support — Discovery pings, since they contain the full public ID of the heard repeater, now bypass all duplicate repeater and display limitation logic and will associate with the appropriate repeater (and count towards leaderboard statistics).
  • New Map Layer: "Adv. Repeater Coverage" — Displays connections to pings using their corresponding colours instead of just blue (green for bidirectional, orange for TX, etc.).
  • Bug Report/Feature Request — Added ability to create a bug report or feature request from within the "About" window on any page.
  • Repeater Staleness — Added ability to allow regional administrators to adjust the amount of time before a repeater is considered stale, replacing the default of 24 hours.
  • Maintenance Mode — Added ability to display a warning notification to website visitors when planned maintenance is expected. Added ability to fully shut down the site and wardriving app if needed with clear messages explaining why.

docs.title: 🔐 Administrative Tools
docs.text:
• Self-Service Passwords — Region administrators can now updates their passwords directly within the admin portal.
  • Geofencing Adjustments — Admins now have the ability to modify region radii or boundary polygons directly from the region administration page.
  • History — Admins now see the history of what changes were made to their region.

docs.title: 🛠️ Improvements & Fixes
docs.text:
• UI/UX Tweaks — Refined various interface elements for a smoother navigation experience.
  • Bug Fixes — Squashed some minor bugs to improve overall platform stability.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Added a Noise Floor graph tab — This is a trial feature, so if it isn't useful it will be removed. Please share your feedback on the noise floor graphs. (Thanks @Peter La)
  • Added light mode under Settings.
  • Submit Feedback button now auto-populates with your companion name.
  • Switch between online and offline mode without disconnecting your companion. Offline sessions still appear under Settings for later upload, same as before. (Thanks @ded (SalishMesh))
  • Added imperial unit option for the app.

docs.title: 🔧 Improvements
docs.text:
• Added additional debug logging around startup checks.
  • Map now defaults to last known location while GPS is loading.
  • Removed a large amount of orphaned code that was no longer in use.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed a race condition on Android that caused startup checks to fail.
  • Fixed an issue that caused excessive Hive writes.
  • Fixed a crash in certain app services when receiving non-UTF-8 characters.
  • Carpeater filter was not being applied to TX responses. Thanks to whoever reported this.
  • Fixed #74: Lower text pop-ups no longer display behind the Android navigation bar (Thanks @saylese).
  • (Android) Resolved Auto audio ducking issue.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text: • New authentication methods and trust levels as mentioned in general.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed a race condition when detecting active internet after returning from the background.
  • Stale repeaters now properly sync between the app and the MeshMapper website (Thanks @ded (SalishMesh)).
  • Another pass at fixing the Android nav bar overlapping popups (Thanks @saylese).

docs.title: ⚡ Improvements
docs.text: • Noise floor graph now updates live without needing to close and reopen it.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Unified Multiregion Architecture — Regions that are part of a multiregion group are now much more deeply linked together. The backend now treats groups as cohesive entities while maintaining the flexibility of individual region databases.
  • Grouped Admin Panel — Administrators managing regions that are part of a multiregion group now have a single, centralized portal to oversee all regions within their group simultaneously.
  • Multiregion Leaderboards — Multiregions now display leaderboards and will contribute as a group to the global leaderboard.

docs.title: 🛠️ Improvements & Fixes
docs.text:
• Global Duplicate Detection — Overhauled API logic to perform duplicate repeater scans across the entire group rather than just the individual region. This ensures that a repeater is recognized as the same entity regardless of which region's observers hear it (as long as those regions share a group).
  • Map Consolidation — To reduce confusion, individual region maps that are part of a group have been deprecated. Navigating to a specific region URL will now automatically redirect to the Grouped Map, providing a "single source of truth" for that mesh.

docs.title: 🔮 Looking Ahead
docs.text: • Automated Clustering — This lays the groundwork for automatic region grouping. In the future, MeshMapper may suggest (or automatically perform) region grouping when a repeater from "Region A" is consistently heard by an observer in "Region B."

docs.title: 🧪 Hybrid Mode (EXPERIMENTAL)
docs.text:
• New mode available as a toggle in Settings. When enabled, the Active button is replaced with a Hybrid button. (Huge kudos to @HerculesMulligan for the idea!)
  • Alternates between Discovery and Active pings each interval.
  • Timing: At a 15s interval, each ping type fires every 30s (alternating), resulting in 50% less TX flood airtime compared to Active Mode at the same interval.
  • Please test and report back with your findings!

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Auto-reconnect to device on Bluetooth disconnect. This addresses a common issue on Apple devices where unplugging power would kill wardriving sessions. The app now automatically retries the connection and resumes the last active mode. (Thanks @FLAN-MAN)
  • Tap the version number to copy it to your clipboard. (Thanks @Dharmansible)

docs.title: 🔧 Improvements
docs.text:
• Debug log files on the Settings tab now display timestamps instead of raw filenames.
  • Removed the exit button from map controls; map can now only be maximized or minimized. (Thanks @d.blair)
  • Resolved two issues flagged during Apple App Store review in preparation for official submission.
  • General code cleanup and stability improvements.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text: • Fixed the noise floor graph freezing and no longer updating after a period of time. (Thanks @ka.RFz (YVR))

docs.title: ⚡ Improvements
docs.text:
• Connection may now take a few seconds longer on initial setup. The app now scans all 40 channel slots for an existing wardriving channel before creating one, preventing duplicate channels from being added to your device.
  • Discovery pings now behave consistently with TX pings. A grey marker is placed on the map as soon as a discovery request is sent, and changes to purple when repeater responses are heard.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed incorrect repeater responses for discovery pings showing up on the noise floor graph.
  • Fixed grey discovery pings (no repeater responses) not appearing on the map.
  • Fixed the user marker not staying centred when auto-follow + heading mode were enabled, especially with the control panel maximized.
  • Fixed a timing issue in hybrid mode that stopped alternating TX and discovery pings from firing at the 15s interval (Thanks @HerculesMulligan).
  • Fixed a visual timer sync issue in hybrid mode that caused the button to flash orange for 1 second.
  • Fixed missing status indicator when sending discovery pings.
  • Fixed map rotation animations to be smoother.

docs.title: 📱 MeshMapper Native App v1.0.0 (1770696177)
docs.text:
Required Update

Please update your app. This update is required to prepare for the final 1.0.0 release.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Cancel Button — Added a cancel button for BLE scanning.
  • Interactive Repeater IDs — Repeater IDs in TX, RX, and Discovery ping results are now tappable, showing the name of known repeaters in a popup.
    • Update: The popup now also shows the distance to each repeater.

docs.title: ⚡ Improvements
docs.text:
• Map Preferences — Centre position, heading, and rotate lock settings are now remembered between sessions.
  • Active Mode — Now includes heartbeat support.
  • Connectivity & Resilience —
    • Replaced the internet check with a graceful fallback if the app can't reach the MeshMapper API during BLE connect.
    • Improved resilience around Hive-backed API queue storage to prevent app freezes from data corruption.
    • More robust failed API handling.
    • Debug log uploads now chunk larger files for reliable delivery.
  • iOS Background Modes — Removed bluetooth-peripheral from UIBackgroundModes, as MeshMapper operates solely as a BLE central.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed certain RX packets being dropped due to content filter rules.
  • Fixed an issue with Android notifications.
  • Fixed capitalization issues.

docs.title: 🚀 MeshMapper Update - Admin Tools & Map Enhancements (February 12, 2026)
✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Detailed vs. Simplified Map Views — Added a toggle in Settings to switch between "Detailed" (full grid resolution) and "Simplified" (clustered repeaters, optimized grid) modes. Simplified mode significantly improves performance on slower devices. Simplified mode is now the default.
  • Discord Integration — Region administrators can now link their Discord! This will allow administrators to receive notifications about their region from the MeshMapper bot (for example, when a new repeater has sent an advert and its ID is now in collision). Find this setting in the "User Settings" tab of the admin panel.
  • Administration Popup — A new "Administration" option on the map displays the region's administrators and contact information, making it easier for visitors to reach out to local maintainers. Visitors can send messages to the administrators through the Discord integration/MeshMapper bot here, if there is at least one region administrator with their Discord linked and that notification type is enabled.
  • Region Message — Administrators can now set a custom message (e.g., local Discord server invite, website link) that appears to visitors in the Administration popup. This allows administrators to direct map visitors to local resources.
  • Disable Duplicate ID Detection — Added the ability for region admins to opt-out of strict duplicate ID detection. This allows regions to visualize all data even if repeater IDs collide. Read about this in more detail here.
  • Onboarding Status — New regions pending onboarding will display in the Maps dropdown with a landing page indicating that onboarding is pending. Optionally linking ones Discord with the onboarding form allows for automated status updates sent directly from the MeshMapper bot.
  • Users — Region administrators now have the ability to create Users. Creating a User allows you the ability to combine multiple companions under a single "primary" companion, which will aggregate a users statistics and contributions under that single companion on the leaderboard. This is also the first stepping stone into a planned portal that will allow end-users the ability to manage their own contributions, see their own detailed statistics, etc.

docs.title: 🛠️ Improvements
docs.text:
• Multiregion Management — Regions within a multiregion group can now be managed independently, giving local admins more control while remaining part of the larger group.
  • Admin Panel Enhancements —
    • Improved filtering and sorting capabilities for data management and export.
    • Added tool parity for multiregion administrators, ensuring they have access to the same maintenance utilities as single-region admins.
  • Onboarding Checks — Strengthened the validation logic during the region onboarding process to ensure configuration accuracy.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text: • Admin Panel — Various bug fixes and stability improvements within the Admin Panel.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Notes for Repeaters and Companions — Added ability to add notes to repeaters and companions in the admin panels. (Thanks Noodles) See More
  • Lock Repeater GPS — Added option in admin panels to lock repeater GPS coordinates to prevent adverts from updating the location. (Thanks jstockton) See More
  • Social Media Links — Administrators can now add social media links to the "Region Info" page of their regions map. (Thanks bbrodka) See More
  • Offline Observer Notifications — Administrators can now opt-in to Discord notifications when an observer hasn't sent data to MeshMapper within the Stale Repeater Age time set on the admin page. (Thanks mrzarquon) Read more about it here

docs.title: 🛠️ Improvements
docs.text:
• Nomenclature — Changed the "Administration" option on a regions map to "Region Info". Changed "Contacts" to "Companions" in admin panels.
  • GPS Validation — Added better GPS coordinates validation on manual edits of repeaters.
  • Neighbour Cleanup — Improved neighbour cleanup logic to account for when repeater ID's get repurposed, or the repeater relocates.
  • DISC Pings — Enhanced DISC ping association with repeaters to better handle if repeater gets relocated (association will now break).
  • Wardriving Updates — Various adjustments to API's and map tile service for Wardriving app.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Map Revamp — Added new Sidebar/Tray view mode! Displays grid square/ping/repeater data in a whole new way, with support for desktop and mobile. Legacy "popup mode" is still available through the settings. (Thanks @HerculesMulligan)
  • Grid Summary — Changes to grid square summary including addition of horizontal bar graph representing coverage.
  • Repeater Summary — Updated repeater summary view including new coverage scatter plot. Better visualize repeater performance!
  • Regions/Scopes — Added administrator control over a regions Scope, if used (Mobile app functionality will be available with v1.1.0)
  • Regional Wardriving Enforcement — Administrators can now enforce Hybrid mode and configure minimum interval settings for wardrivers in their region. Useful if a regions mesh is suffering from dropped packets due to load. (Mobile app functionality will be available with v1.1.0)

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text: • Heatmap — Fixed ping/repeater clickthrough when heatmap layer was enabled (Thanks @NoahK)

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• New Admin Panels — Completely redesigned admin panel UI - and now with mobile support!
  • Repeater Neighbours — When selected on a repeater, clicking another repeater under "Neighbours" will display the neighbour on the map and draw neighbour lines.

  • Leaderboard Links — Clicking on a repeater on a regional or global leaderboard will open a new tab and take you directly to that repeater. (Thanks Vgo)

  • Neighbour Boundries — A new "Neighbour Zone Boundries" map layer will expose neighbouring regions boundries. (Thanks davidjay)

  • Neighbour Boundries — Regional administrators can now see neighbouring regions boundries when editing their own from within the admin panel.

  • Duplicate Repeater Cleanup — Instead of a hardcoded 3 days, repeaters in active ID collision will delete themselves after 3x the number of hours set as the regions Stale Repeater Age. (Thanks to many)

docs.title: February 26, 2026
docs.text: 🎉 MeshMapper App v1.1.0

docs.title: New Features
docs.text:
• Regional Flood Scoping: If a region has configured scopes on their repeaters, a MeshMapper regional admin can now set the scope of Active/Hybrid Mode TX pings. This limits flood messages to a specific channel, stopping wardriving pings from flooding past boundaries defined by the local mesh. Discovery pings are unaffected.
  • Scope Indicator: The Regional Configuration card on the Connection tab now shows your active scope (e.g., #ottawa) or "Global" when no scope is set.
  • Anonymous Mode: New privacy option in Settings that renames your device to "Anonymous" for all mesh pings. Other mesh users can no longer see your companion name in wardrive broadcasts. Your device's public key still authenticates your session with the MeshMapper API (geo-auth), but your sessions and pings are not stored with your public key on the server. Toggling while connected automatically disconnects and reconnects with the new identity, switching to the Connection tab so you can see progress. On proper disconnect, the original device name is restored. Persisted across app restarts.
  • Auto Tile Refresh: Map tiles now refresh automatically after a successful upload, so new coverage appears near real-time (around 15s after a ping).
  • Faster Uploads: TX and discovery pings now upload on a predictable 5-second timer, replacing the old batch system (up to 20s worst case).
  • CARpeater Filter Toggle: Disable the RSSI CARpeater filter in Wardrive Settings (with a stern confirmation warning about data integrity consequences).
  • Multi-hop CARpeater Handling: Multi-hop CARpeater packets now strip the CARpeater hop and report coverage from the underlying repeater with null SNR/RSSI, instead of being dropped entirely. Single-hop packets and discovery responses are still dropped. The -30 dBm failsafe is bypassed for packets matching your CARpeater prefix but stays active for everything else.
  • Offline Mode: Option now displayed when zone check fails due to no internet. Start wardriving immediately instead of waiting for connectivity.
  • Admin: Enforced Hybrid Mode: Regional admins can now enforce hybrid mode per-zone through the API. When enabled, hybrid mode is automatically activated and the toggle is locked on the Settings screen with a "Forced Enabled by Regional Admin" label.
  • Admin: Minimum Ping Interval: Regional admins can set a minimum auto-ping interval per-zone. Interval options below the threshold are greyed out in Settings with a "Disabled by Regional Admin" label. If your saved interval is below the new minimum, it gets automatically bumped up on connect.

docs.title: Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed API queue race condition that could cause missed or double-processed items
  • Fixed ping race condition where rapid double-taps could trigger concurrent pings
  • Fixed resource cleanup gaps causing listener leaks from undisposed stream subscriptions and timers
  • Fixed tile refresh race condition causing half-rendered and ghost tiles at certain zoom levels
  • Fixed Light (OSM) and Satellite (ArcGIS) tiles not loading at low zoom on iOS retina displays
  • GITHUB 98: The heard repeaters panel now scrolls and dynamically expands to accommodate larger lists of heard repeaters
  • GITHUB 95: Fixed the IATA popup panel being hidden behind the Android navigation bar
  • Fixed a bug where a stale BLE device name was used instead of the current one
  • CARpeater filter order of operations corrected
  • Fixed RX CARpeater detection checking the wrong hop position. On RX packets, the CARpeater is co-located with the wardriver so it only appears as the last hop (delivery repeater), not the first. The check now correctly evaluates the last hop.

docs.title: Improvements
docs.text:
• In-memory log and map pin lists are now capped (500 for logs/pins, 200 for errors) to prevent memory bloat during long wardriving sessions
  • Replaced force unwraps with safe null checks across connection, ping, and debug services to prevent crashes
  • Empty catch blocks now log errors with descriptive tags instead of silently swallowing exceptions
  • Added a warning dialog before submitting debug logs alerting you that logs contain approximate GPS location history
  • Documented AES-ECB as a MeshCore protocol requirement
  • Removed deprecated method in channel_service
  • Listen windows reduced from 7s to 5s across the board
  • Map zoom range adjusted to 3-17
  • Disconnect now uploads immediately with no delay

🔧 MeshMapper v1.1.1 [HOTFIX]

🐛 Bug Fix

  • Fixed a scope matching issue that caused pings to be sent with the wrong packet type. Zones without a regional scope use #* as a wildcard, but the app only recognized *, which caused it to set the wrong flood scope on the radio. This meant TX pings went out as TransportFlood instead of regular FLOOD.

⚙️ Improvements

  • Debug logs are now enabled by default

  • Log uploads now prompt you to describe what's going on before submitting, making it easier for us to diagnose issues. The upload button has also moved beside "Delete All" under Debug Logs.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• AI MeshMapper Bot — The AI-powered MeshMapper assistant is now public! Have a question? Start a chat from the help icon on any regional map, or tag @MeshMapper in the MeshMapper Discord. The bot is continuously learning and improving (and knows only about Meshmapper).
  • Profiles & Awards — Companion/user profiles and an awards system have been added to MeshMapper! View any profile by clicking a name on a leaderboard or using the "Profiles" search button. Awards are granted automatically for milestones (data points, grid squares, leaderboard rank) or manually by administrators. See the full details and what awards are available to be earned on the Profiles & Awards wiki page.

  • Pending Repeater Approval — Regional administrators can now enable a flag that has new repeaters enter in a "Pending" state, needing approval by a regional administrator before appearing on the map. Pending repeaters have 3x the regions Stale Repeater Timer before being automatically activated, or are deleted if no advert is received within 1x the regions Stale Repeater Timer. A new Discord notification type has been created that will notify regional administrators once a day if the region has any pending repeaters. This is an anti-abuse measure and should be used with caution, as it can cause inaccurate map data. Read about it more here.

  • New Administrators — Regional administrators can now create new regional administrator accounts. Create the account and send the new administrator the registration link to complete their registration. Read about it more here.

  • Top Explorers (Regional) — The Top Explorers leaderboard, which awards a point for being the first to wardrive a grid square, has been added to regional leaderboards. (Thanks ded)

  • Global Leaderboard Additions — The global leaderboard now includes rankings for Most Repeaters, Most Grid Squares, Most Contributors, and Most Data Points across all regions.

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text: • Fixed an issue where the map legend was layered above the help button. (Thanks Alex The Emperor Penguin)

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Effective Coverage Map — A new map layer that visualizes coverage based on effective reach! Read the details here (Thanks HerculesMulligan)
  • Signal Strength Map — A new map layer that color-codes coverage by signal strength (SNR), making it easy to identify strong and weak zones at a glance. Read the details here (Thanks davidjay)
  • In/Out Boundary Labels on Repeaters — The repeaters table in the regional admin panel now indicates whether a repeater is inside or outside the region boundary. (Thanks Michał Konopiński)
  • Public Repeater ID List — A publicly accessible list of a regions repeater ID utilization is now available. Find this on the "Region Info" page of a region and click "Repeater List". (Thanks Michał Konopiński)
  • Bulk Select & Batch Edit/Delete — Regional administrators can now multi-select repeaters and companions in the admin panel for batch editing or batch deletion. (Thanks davidjay)
  • Ping Reassociation Tool — A new maintenance tool in the admin panel allows administrators to reassociate pings to a repeater that was missing from the map at the time of ingestion. The tool analyzes affected pings, detects ID collisions, and handles both standard and DISC pings. (Thanks NoahK)
  • Admin Contact Info — Administrators can now update their contact information directly from the User Settings tab in the admin panel. (Thanks AI7NC)
  • Grant Existing Admins Access to New Regions — When adding a new administrator, if the administrator already exists, access to that region in question will be given to that existing administrator instead of having to create a new account. (Thanks AI7NC)

docs.title: 🔧 Improvements
docs.text: • Repeater Neighbours Fade Effect — When viewing Repeater Neighbour lines, coverage pings now fade into the background to make the neighbour connections more visible and easier to trace. (Thanks EldoonNemar)

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text: • Fixed an issue where search filters, sort order, and pagination were reset after deleting a repeater, companion, or other record in the admin panel. State is now preserved across edit and delete operations. (Thanks davidjay)

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Settings Panel — A new settings panel has been added to the map interface, providing quick access to display preferences, coverage options, and grid transparency controls all in one place.
  • Adjustable Grid Transparency — Users can now adjust the opacity of coverage grid squares directly from the settings panel, with separate sliders for normal and faded opacity levels. (Thanks davidjay)
  • Effective Coverage Colour Spectrum — The effective coverage layer now supports a full-spectrum colour mode (Red → Blue) in addition to the default Red → Green, selectable from the settings panel. (Thanks HerculesMulligan)
  • Active Wardriving Indicators — The main landing page now shows regions with active wardriving sessions as pulsing green icons on the map, making it easy to see where wardrivers are currently collecting data in real time.
  • Consider Failed DISC as DROP — A new region-level setting allows administrators to treat failed discovery (DISC) pings as DROP pings. This functionality is coming to the mobile apps shortly.

  • Bulk Delete on Coverage Table — The coverage data table in the admin panel now supports multi-select with bulk delete. (Thanks davidjay)

  • Hide Dissociated Pings — A new option in the map settings will prevent pings associated with missing/old repeaters from displaying on the map. (Thanks Vjo)

  • Effective Coverage Minimum Sample Size — An option has been added to the map settings that allows for configuring the minimum number of samples/pings required in order to place an effective coverage grid square on the map. (Thanks AI7NC)

  • Selectable Grid Size — Grid square sizes can now be adjusted in the map settings! (Thanks tkelemen)

docs.title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed an issue where spaces/whitespace was allowed within the scopes field, causing an error on the app.

  • Fixed an issue where coverage pings could not be opened on the map from the admin panel when in certain coverage display modes. (Thanks NoahK)

  • Fixed double-encoding issue on Repeaters table in admin panel. (Thanks NoahK)

docs.title: 🚀 MeshMapper Update (March 8, 2026)
docs.text:
Expect Bugs

This release includes significant changes to how MeshMapper handles repeater identification across the entire solution — from data ingestion to map rendering. While extensively tested, edge cases are likely. If you encounter any issues, please report them.

docs.title: ✨ New Features
docs.text:
• Dynamic Hop Bytes Support — The MeshMapper backend and map now fully support 1, 2, and 3-byte repeater hop identification across all features. This requires repeaters and companions running firmware v1.14.0 or newer.

    • Collision detection, coverage mapping, leaderboards, all use the hop byte length
    • The region's Hop Bytes setting is configurable in the region admin panel under Settings

  • Per-Repeater Hop Byte Tracking — MeshMapper now tracks hop bytes on a per-repeater basis. When a repeater is heard with a longer ID than previously seen, its hop bytes value is automatically updated. This allows regions to transition gradually from 1-byte to multi-byte firmware.

  • Smart Collision Detection — Collision detection now uses per-repeater hop bytes to determine if two repeaters are truly in conflict. Two repeaters are only considered colliding if they are indistinguishable at the longer of their two hop byte lengths. For example, AB (1-byte) and AB12 (2-byte) are no longer flagged as duplicates.

  • Hop Bytes Advanced Filter — A new "Hop Bytes" dropdown has been added to the advanced search panel on the map, allowing users to filter repeater markers by their hop byte value (1, 2, or 3).

  • Repeater List Grid Update — The Repeater List grid in Region Info now filters out multi-byte repeaters from 1-byte cells to avoid false "taken" indicators. New 1-byte, 2-byte, and 3-byte repeater lists are available to help regions in coordinating firmware upgrades.

docs.title: March 17, 2026
docs.text:
🎉 MeshMapper v1.2.0 is here!

This is a big one. Three headline features, a redesigned UI, and a mountain of fixes.

📡 Highlights

🔢 Multi-Byte Path Hash Support

MeshMapper now fully supports MeshCore firmware v1.14.0+, expanding repeater IDs from 256 (1-byte) up to 65K (2-byte) or 16M (3-byte). A long time coming! Hex badges with dynamic sizing, square repeater markers on the map, admin-enforced byte modes per region, passive multi-byte detection, and automatic conflict remediation for duplicate IDs.

🎯 Trace Mode

TX pings can now target a specific repeater using MeshCore's trace path command instead of broadcasting to all repeaters. Designed for testing a single repeater's reach and coverage. Failed pings are not recorded as no-coverage since other repeaters may still serve that area.

🗺️ Discovery Drop Reporting

Failed discovery requests can now be reported as failed pings to help map dead zones and noise floor gaps. Enable it in Settings (off by default). Failed discoveries show up as red markers on the map and noise floor chart. Regional admins can enforce this for their whole region once nodes are updated.

✨ More New Features

  • Repeater Marker Rotation — Markers now rotate with the map and stay upright
  • Auto-Stop After Idle — Auto-ping stops after 30 min of no movement, saving battery and radio (default: ON)
  • Repeater Picker for Trace Mode — Browse nearby repeaters by name, hex ID, distance, and active status
  • Live Repeater Snapshot — Top 3 responded repeaters from your last ping, updating live as a map overlay

🔧 Changes

  • Redesigned the Connection tab for clarity
  • Sound moved from control panel to Settings
  • Offline data session management always visible under Settings
  • TX/RX and Trace now have independent byte settings (TX: 1/2/3, Trace: 1/2/4)
  • Trace bytes auto-sync from TX on connect (3-byte TX maps to 4-byte Trace)
  • "Path Bytes" renamed to "TX Bytes," new "Trace Bytes" dropdown with info button
  • Trace target hex input adapts to Trace Bytes (2, 4, or 8 hex characters)

⬆️ Improvements

  • Auto-ping shows "Skipped Xs" instead of generic countdown when movement is insufficient
  • Hybrid mode defaults to enabled for new and existing users
  • Minimum ping distance now configurable for TX and discovery pings
  • Option to keep #wardriving channel after exiting a session (#149, @skye)
